entry.title: Cloud Identity Services Trial Admin Console Returns forbidden.html
entry.content:
I created a new SAP BTP Trial account and subscribed to Cloud Identity Services (default plan).

The tenant was created successfully and I can log in. Profile Management works correctly.

However, when I open the Administration Console (either from BTP Cockpit → Go to Application or directly via https://.trial-accounts.ondemand.com/admin), I am redirected to:

/admin/forbidden.html

Additional information:

  • User has Subaccount Administrator role.

  • Trust Configuration is healthy.

  • Administration Console application is shown as ACTIVE for the user.

  • Profile Management works.

  • Trial account was created on June 10, 2026.

Expected result:
Administration Console opens successfully.

Actual result:
forbidden.html is displayed.

Has anyone seen this with a newly created trial tenant, and is there a way to repair the administrator assignment without recreating the tenant?

entry.title: SAP Transformation Days 2026: A Global Success Story in Cloud ERP Transformation
entry.content:
The SAP Transformation Day series has established itself as a cornerstone event for organizations navigating their journey to SAP Cloud ERP. Co-hosted by SAP SE and regional User Groups since 2025 and continuing throughout 2026, this initiative has successfully brought together over 1,100 customers across multiple continents, creating a powerful platform for knowledge sharing, strategic guidance, and community collaboration.

Each SAP Transformation Day provides a comprehensive overview of SAP's strategy and roadmap for a successful transition to SAP Cloud ERP. Participants gain a clear understanding of how SAP's Cloud ERP portfolio – including both Private Cloud and Public Cloud deployment options – fits into the broader SAP’s strategy and what this means for their organization's digital transformation journey.

The SAP Transformation Day 2026 has already made its mark across diverse regions, demonstrating SAP's commitment to supporting customers worldwide in their digital transformation journey:
Asia Pacific, Germany,  Latin America, Nordics, South Africa, United Kingdom, and a special Pre-Conference session at SAPPHIRE with ASUG.

With upcoming events scheduled in the Netherlands , Mexico, Columbia the momentum continues to build, bringing SAP's Cloud ERP strategy directly to customers in their local markets.

What Makes SAP Transformation Day Unique?

Strategic Partnership with User Groups

The co-hosting model with regional User Groups ensures that these events are not just top-down in The event also featured testimonials from User Group members who have already completed their transformations, offering attendees real-world lessons learned and best practices directly from peers who have gone through the journey. The opening sessions consistently highlight the critical role User Groups play in influencing SAP's roadmap and commercial decisions, demonstrating a genuine commitment to customer-driven innovation.

Comprehensive Coverage of Cloud ERP Strategy

Each SAP Transformation Day provides participants with a complete understanding of:

  • SAP Cloud ERP Strategy within the broader SAP Business Suite context
  • Portfolio Overview of Cloud ERP offerings including key deployment options (Private and Public Cloud) and solution highlights
  • Functional Differentiation between SAP Cloud ERP and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise
  • Transformation and Migration Approaches tailored to different organizational needs
  • Customer Experience
  • Maintenance Strategy and Timelines for informed decision-making
  • Special Programs including Safekeeper, ERP Transition Options, and Compatibility Scope
  • Customer Experience: Information and feedback sharing from existing SAP S/4HANA Cloud and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition customers

These events are designed to equip participants with:

  • Strategic insights into SAP's Cloud ERP vision
  • Practical guidance on transformation approaches (Brownfield vs. Greenfield)
  • Best practices from successful customer journeys
  • Understanding of AI capabilities and business value
  • Clear timelines and maintenance strategies
  • Change management considerations for sustainable adoption

The Power of Real Customer Experiences

One of the most valuable aspects of SAP Transformation Day is the inclusion of customer stories and first-hand experiences. These sessions go beyond theoretical frameworks to share:

  • Decision criteria for different transformation approaches
  • Key value drivers that made the difference
  • Practical lessons learned
  • Honest discussions about what works well and what challenges to anticipate

Looking Ahead

As SAP Transformation Day series continues to expand to new regions and formats, including the integration with major SAP events like SAPPHIRE, the series reinforces SAP's commitment to transparent communication and customer enablement.

The success of reaching over 1,100 customers across several regions demonstrates the strong demand for clear, comprehensive guidance on Cloud ERP transformation. More importantly, it showcases the power of collaboration between SAP and its User Group community in creating truly valuable customer experiences.

Join the Journey

Whether you're just beginning to explore SAP Cloud ERP options or are actively planning your transformation, SAP Transformation Day offers the strategic insights, practical guidance, and community connections you need to succeed.

Stay tuned for announcements about upcoming events in your region, and join the growing community of organizations confidently moving forward on their Cloud ERP transformation journey.

For more information about upcoming SAP Transformation Day events or to learn more about SAP Cloud ERP, connect with your local SAP User Group or contact your SAP representative.

entry.title: Re: REHIRE WITH OLD EMPLOYMENT REPLICATION TO ECP
entry.content:
@krcc1 A common use case for adding the OnboardingInfo object to an integration is when using Rehire with Old Employment. This object ensures rehires don't replicate downstream before completing Manage Pending Hires. It leverages the hiring status within the OnboardingInfo object.

You need onboardinginfo only for rehire candidates - all others HiringNotComplete flag should suffice

https://help.sap.com/docs/successfactors-onboarding/implementing-onboarding/onboarding-info-object

Thanks,

Gopi

entry.title: Re: How to create a portal memo in a C4C ticket with the SAP ODATA API
entry.content:
Thanks this was really useful.  Additional tip: you can combine the calls into one to minimise the number of API requests being made.

POST to ActivityCollection:
https://my.______crm.ondemand.com/sap/c4c/odata/v1/c4codataapi/ActivityCollection

{
	"TypeCode": "2574",
	"SubjectName": "My test subject",
	"LifeCycleStatusCode": "1",
	"InitiatorCode": "1",
	"ActivityText": [
		{
			"Text": "Testing 123",
			"TypeCode": "10011"
		}
	],
	"ActivityBusinessTransactionDocumentReference": [
		{
			"ID": "<ticket id>",
			"TypeCode": "118",
			"RoleCode": "1"
		}
	]
}

entry.title: Unlocking the NPP: PayTo
entry.content:
Welcome to the fourth and final installment on our blog series on the New Payments Platform (“NPP”) in Australia.

In this series, we have explored the following: 

  1. Unlocking the NPP: SAP Capabilities for Faster, Smarter Payments
  2. Unlocking the NPP: Payments overview
  3. Unlocking the NPP: PayID
  4. Unlocking the NPP: PayTo — you are here

Now let's turn to PayTo, the smarter way to manage recurring payments.

Managing recurring payments and client authorisations can feel like navigating a maze of paperwork, delayed confirmations, and frustrated clients. The traditional direct debit system has long been a necessary but cumbersome tool, but what if there was a better way, one that puts control in your clients' hands while streamlining your payment operations?

The answer is PayTo: Australia's next-generation payment solution built on the New Payments Platform (NPP). This innovative system is transforming how businesses request and receive payments.

What is PayTo?

PayTo is a digital payment agreement that allows businesses to request funds directly from a customer's bank account with their pre-authorisation. Unlike traditional direct debits that operate behind the scenes, PayTo puts transparency and control front and center.

  • Customers can use their PayID or BSB and account number to make payments.
  • Merchants and businesses can set up a PayTo agreement for one-off, ad hoc, or regular payments.
  • The PayTo agreement includes all relevant payment terms, which must be authorised through the customer's banking channel.
  • Customers gain greater flexibility to view and manage all their payments in one place.

Steps involved in the PayTo process:

PayTo agreement creation: Businesses set up a PayTo agreement(PayTo Mandate) through their financial institution or payment service provider, recording the customer's authorisation for future payments.

Approval:  The customer receives a real-time notification through their secure banking channel to approve or reject the agreement.

Management:  Once approved, both parties can manage the agreement through their respective platforms, with payments initiated according to the pre-authorised terms.

PayTo agreement creation

When a customer signs up for a PayTo arrangement, the agreement creation flows through the NPP infrastructure, involving the business, a PayTo Provider, the payer and their respective bank. The diagram below illustrates how this mandate creation works end-to-end.

 
PayTo payments initiation

Once a PayTo agreement is in place, the business(biller) can initiate payments at any time according to the agreed terms. The payment flows through the NPP infrastructure using ISO 20022 messaging standards, ensuring real-time clearing and settlement between the Payer's and Payee's banks.

 What information is exchanged?

PayTo uses ISO 20022 message standards to structure the data flowing between parties. The table below provides an overview of the information sent and received when creating a PayTo agreement, initiating a payment and processing the the bank statement.

Note: The fields above are based on the ISO 20022 messaging standard. In practice, each bank defines its own specific requirements for both payment file content and bank statement. So the exact fields, formats, and optionality may vary depending on your financial institution.

Why businesses are wanting to move to PayTo?

PayTo offers compelling advantages over traditional payment methods.

Real-time authorisation: No more waiting days to confirm whether a client has accepted a payment arrangement. Clients receive instant notifications and can approve agreements on the spot, dramatically reducing setup time.

Flexible payment structures: PayTo supports fixed amounts, variable payments, one-time transactions, and recurring payments, making it adaptable to virtually any billing model.
Simpler identification: Integration with PayID means clients can authorise payments using their Australian Business Number, email address or mobile number, eliminating the need to share BSB and account numbers.
Security and compliance built in:  Every agreement requires explicit customer authorisation through their bank's secure channel. Customers have full visibility and can pause or cancel agreements at any time which helps build trust and significantly reduces the potential for disputes.
 
The bigger picture

PayTo isn't just a technical upgrade, it's a shift in the relationship between businesses and their clients. By giving customers real visibility and control over payment agreements, businesses can reduce friction, build stronger trust, and spend less time chasing confirmations.

If your business relies on recurring payments, PayTo is worth exploring. It's the direct debit experience, completely reimagined for the digital age.

Interested in exploring PayTo with SAP?

SAP is actively engaging with customers as part of a dedicated PayTo Customer Engagement Initiative. This is your opportunity to work directly with SAP to shape how PayTo is implemented, share your requirements, and influence the solution roadmap.

If your organisation is looking to modernise its payment processes and wants to be part of the conversation, we would love to hear from you. 

Join the PayTo Customer Engagement Initiative Registration closes on the 21st of June 2026.

entry.title: Holiday Season Readiness Program - Proactively Preparing for Peak with Confidence
entry.content:
E-commerce is evolving fast, and the past holiday seasons have shown that peak readiness is no longer just about handling traffic spikes.  It is about delivering reliable, intelligent, and seamless shopping experiences at scale.
 
After the already impressive and successful years 2022 and 2023, in 2024, SAP Commerce Cloud processed US$12.2 billion in GMV during Cyber Week with 100% uptime, while Cyber Monday alone delivered a 71.8% year-over-year GMV increase. At the same time, global online holiday sales reached US$1.2 trillion.
In 2025, that momentum continued. HSRP reporting showed 100% infrastructure uptime, US$17.8 billion in GMV, and 40% year-over-year growth during the holiday season, with AI- and agent-assisted experiences increasing influence.
 
Consumer expectations are also changing. SAP notes that 58% of Gen Z and millennials would trust an AI agent to compare prices and recommend products, while 72% of consumers say they stay loyal only to brands that meet their needs in the moment. This makes holiday success increasingly dependent on performance, personalization, discoverability, and post-purchase reliability.
To help customers prepare for this environment, SAP is launching the Holiday Season Readiness Program (HSRP) 2026. The program supports customers with proactive planning, environment analysis, scaling recommendations, readiness guidance, and cross-team coordination ahead of major holiday events. As with previous years, HSRP is focused on preparation and readiness of SAP's customers.
 
Let’s continue to make the next holiday season even more successful.
 
Customer registrations to this year's program start on June 29, 2026.

 

Key events covered in HSRP 2026

The holiday season isn't just one weekend. Traffic rises earlier, peaks faster, lasts longer, and is increasingly shaped by mobile shopping, AI-driven discovery, and high-intent buying moments. HSRP 2026 is designed to help customers prepare for the most important seasonal events with confidence.  With early planning and close coordination, we can help make your 2026 peak season more stable, more predictable, and more successful.

HSRP 2026 events with highest expected traffic:

  • Singles’ Day - November 11, 2026
  • El Buen Fin - November 13 - 16, 2026
  • Black Friday Cyber Monday - November 27 - 30, 2026 (followed by Cyber Week)
  • Christmas & Holiday Period
SAP is aware that many customers also run important promotions outside these dates and monitors with highest awareness 24/7. However, if you are planning a major event beyond the official HSRP calendar, please contact your Customer Success Manager or write to SAP-HSRP@sap.com so we can align on the right preparation approach. Events outside the official HSRP scope follow the standard surge-event support process.
 
 

Who can participate?

HSRP 2026 is open to SAP Commerce Cloud customers planning promotions or expecting increased traffic during key holiday periods such as Singles’ Day, El Buen Fin, Black Friday/Cyber Monday, or Christmas. 
The program is especially relevant for live customers and for customers planning to go live before the holiday season. While HSRP has traditionally supported B2C businesses, B2B and B2B2C customers expecting seasonal demand peaks are also encouraged to participate.
 

What support does the program offer?

HSRP 2026 helps customers prepare early, reduce avoidable risk, and make better decisions before peak traffic begins. It is a readiness program, focused on preparation rather than a different support model during the live sales events.

Customers who participate receive:

  • Environment analysis and scaling recommendations: SAP uses customer input, historical traffic patterns, and internal tooling to assess whether preventive actions or temporary scaling may be needed ahead of peak events.
  • Preparation checklists and readiness guidance: Customers receive structured guidance covering preparation activities, communication, technical checks, and planning steps ahead of major seasonal events.
  • Targeted recommendations and relevant SAP resources: Participants are guided to the SAP resources, best practices, and readiness materials most relevant to their expected seasonal demand.
Participation is free of charge. Customers can still run seasonal events without HSRP, but enrollment is strongly recommended because it allows SAP to evaluate demand earlier and prepare more effectively.
 

Why is enrollment important?

SAP cannot predict a customer’s promotion calendar, traffic expectations, campaign intensity, or infrastructure needs without customer input. Enrollment gives SAP earlier visibility into your plans so teams can review your landscape, assess risks, and coordinate recommendations in advance.
 

HSRP 2026 timeline

Registration is open from June 29. 
Once a customer enrolls, SAP begins the readiness phase. Based on the current HSRP process, this includes communication, environment reviews, scaling evaluation, and broader coordination across the teams involved in holiday preparation.
Review results are communicated in the months of September and October, on-time before the Holiday Season starts.
 
 

Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)

What if I am not sure how much traffic to expect?
You should still enroll. HSRP uses customer input, historical patterns, and internal readiness tooling to help assess demand and build in reasonable buffers where needed.

What if I am still in project phase but plan to go live before the holiday season?
You can still participate. The program is intended primarily for live customers and for those planning to go live before the peak holiday period. If your go-live is delayed, that does not create an issue for SAP.

What if I miss the registration deadline or I am not yet sure whether I will run a campaign?
You can still run seasonal events without HSRP. However, events outside the program or communicated late follow the standard surge-event support process. Non-enrolled customers should proactively raise scaling requests if needed.

What if my promotion is outside the official HSRP dates?
That is still possible. Please flag the event through your Customer Success Manager or via SAP-HSRP@sap.com so SAP can review whether additional preparation is advisable. Events outside the official HSRP dates follow the standard process.

How will I be informed if SAP identifies changes needed in my environment?
If SAP identifies a need to increase infrastructure, the team will proactively contact you through a customer-facing service request ticket. Enrolled customers therefore receive earlier visibility into planned readiness actions. We will keep you uptodate on a high level of our progress.

How can I ask questions or provide feedback?
You can contact your Customer Success Partner or write directly to SAP-HSRP@sap.com. The HSRP mailbox is listed as a direct contact channel in the current program materials.
How can I receive system notifications for my SAP Commerce Cloud solution?
Customers should subscribe to the SAP Cloud System Notification Subscription (CSNS) service so key stakeholders receive important system notifications for their landscape.
 
For more information about HSRP 2026 or to express interest in participation to our success stories, please contact your SAP representative or write to SAP-HSRP@sap.com. Find our success stories with our customers here: https://www.sap.com/germany/blogs/holidays-come-early-for-retailers

entry.title: SAP Cloud Support Organization: A Guide to Roles and Responsibilities
entry.content:
Overview

In my years of engaging with SAP customers, I have consistently observed a recurring pain point: many customers, even after subscribing to SAP cloud services, remain unclear about which SAP personnel to contact and for what purposes. Unlike B2C interactions — where a consumer can simply open an app and reach a support agent — enterprise software is an entirely different landscape. SAP's organization is large and multifaceted, spanning numerous modules and specialized domains, which makes navigating the right contacts genuinely challenging.

This guide provides a clear breakdown of SAP's internal roles and responsibilities, enabling you to leverage SAP's resources more effectively.

Overview

To address the demands of cloud-era subscription models, SAP has built a multi-tiered customer support framework that spans the entire customer lifecycle. From pre-sales account executives to post-contract Onboarding Advisors and Customer Success Managers, through to delivery and operations roles such as ECS, TQM, and SAP Support, and extending to optional value-added services like Preferred Success and CAS — each role carries a distinct mandate, and all operate in coordinated collaboration to drive customer adoption and sustained value realization.

The subscription model inherently demands a more granular, continuous approach to customer support. From initial order placement through go-live to renewal, different roles must be engaged and orchestrated at each stage.

1. Sales / Industry Account Executive (IAE) — Included

Scope of Responsibility Responsible for ensuring customers procure the right solutions and services aligned to their business requirements. The IAE serves as the customer's primary point of contact at SAP throughout the entire cloud journey.

Engagement Phase Pre-sales through contract execution, and subsequently during change request phases.

Key Deliverables Contract execution, solution selection guidance, change order coordination.

Customer Interaction Model Engages directly with senior stakeholders and business decision-makers to ensure commercial agreements are properly structured and executed. In the absence of a CSM, the IAE may also coordinate and initiate the Onboarding process.

2. CSM / D-CSM (Customer Success Manager / Digital Customer Success Manager) — Included

Scope of Responsibility The CSM role is broadly consistent with its counterpart at other cloud vendors: it is designed to manage the customer lifecycle post-contract. It is important to distinguish this from the sales function — while the IAE is also involved across the full customer lifecycle, the sales focus is anchored to commercial milestones. Everything beyond that — product usage guidance, billing clarification, best practices, and internal resource coordination — falls within the CSM's remit.

That said, not every customer is assigned a CSM. CSM allocation is typically threshold-based, tied to subscription value within a given product line. To illustrate (figures are hypothetical): if a customer has invested €2M in SAP BTP and only €100K in SAP SuccessFactors, they would likely receive a dedicated BTP CSM but not a SuccessFactors CSM.

A note on Digital CSMs (D-CSMs): these individuals typically manage a portfolio of over one hundred accounts. While they perform the same functional role, the depth of product knowledge and service intensity they can offer is necessarily more limited than a dedicated CSM. If you encounter one, they remain a valid escalation path — they will route you to the appropriate internal resource.

Engagement Phase Post-contract, spanning the full subscription lifecycle — go-live, ongoing operations, and renewal.

Key Deliverables Internal resource coordination, customer satisfaction management, Onboarding and implementation consultant engagement, renewal facilitation.

Customer Interaction Model Regular engagement with customer leadership; coordination of internal SAP resources including Onboarding Advisors and implementation consultants; central point of orchestration when customers encounter escalations or complex challenges.

Note: In certain SAP documentation, the CSM role may also be referred to as E-CSP (Enterprise Cloud Success Partner). The S-CSP designation refers to a product line-specific variant.

3. Onboarding Advisor / Onboarding Consultant — Included

Scope of Responsibility Part of SAP's Customer Service Delivery (CSD) organization, Onboarding Advisors are product line specialists responsible for introducing customers to relevant services, guiding initial provisioning and activation, ensuring timely service delivery, and driving early customer satisfaction.

Engagement Phase Early post-contract period (the Onboarding phase), with primary impact in the weeks immediately following contract signature.

Key Deliverables Product onboarding orientation (how to submit tickets, manage S-users, register for webinars); provisioning walkthroughs for BTP, BDC, and AI-related services (BTP Cockpit, Integration Suite, data warehousing, HANA, SAP Build, etc. — typically 1–1.5 hours per topic); confirmation that customers can successfully access relevant services for development and consumption.

Customer Interaction Model Guidance and enablement delivered via a structured series of online sessions. Engagements may be initiated by the CSM, the sales team, or the customer directly; Onboarding Advisors also proactively reach out to customer IT managers. The engagement model is intentionally flexible. Post-go-live issues are subsequently handed off to other SAP resources (e.g., implementation consultants) coordinated by the CSM — Onboarding Advisors do not maintain ongoing tracking after this point.

4. ECS (Enterprise Cloud Services) — Included

ECS comprises three dedicated roles, primarily engaged during the delivery and operational phases of private cloud ERP environments. This function is principally oriented toward customers running SAP S/4HANA Private Cloud. If your questions relate to BTP — such as subaccount creation, identity provider integration, or SAP Analytics Cloud provisioning — ECS is not the right contact. For those topics, engage your SAP AE, CSM, or Onboarding Advisor.

Project Lead (PL)

Scope of Responsibility Responsible for ensuring SAP builds and delivers the system in accordance with what was sold and contractually agreed. Engaged during the system build phase, and re-engaged if changes or extensions are required subsequently.

Engagement Phase System delivery / build phase (time-bound engagement).

Key Deliverables On-scope system delivery, change request execution.

Customer Interaction Model Direct collaboration with the customer throughout the system build phase.

Client Delivery Manager (CDM)

Scope of Responsibility Owns the operational phase of the delivered system and serves as the single point of contact for all matters related to S/4HANA configuration and ongoing operations.

Engagement Phase System operations phase (long-term, ongoing).

Key Deliverables Operational coordination, issue escalation management.

Customer Interaction Model Primary ongoing contact for the operational phase; maintains continuous customer engagement.

Technical Service Manager (TSM)

Scope of Responsibility A seasoned SAP technical consultant who works in close partnership with the CDM and serves as the primary technical contact for all infrastructure and technology-related topics.

Engagement Phase System operations phase (long-term, ongoing).

Key Deliverables Technical issue resolution, infrastructure support.

Customer Interaction Model Addresses technical and infrastructure-related matters in close coordination with the CDM.

5. TQM (Technical Quality Manager) / Launch Advisor (LA) — Billable

Scope of Responsibility The Launch Advisor is a senior, multi-disciplinary expert — often described as a "black belt" — who helps customers plan and navigate each phase of their cloud journey. This includes coaching and upskilling customers and their organizations, proactively identifying risks and issues before they materialize, and supporting their resolution.

Engagement Phase Can be engaged at any point in the process for as long as needed; earlier engagement consistently yields better outcomes.

Key Deliverables Risk identification and mitigation, project planning guidance, organizational capability development.

Customer Interaction Model Procured on demand; delivers highly personalized, hands-on expert engagement. SAP's experience consistently shows that having a Launch Advisor is a material success factor for complex implementations.

Note: In other SAP product lines and service frameworks — particularly MaxAttention and ActiveAttention — the equivalent role is referred to as TQM (Technical Quality Manager).

6. SAP Support (Standard Support) — Included

Scope of Responsibility SAP Support handles product-specific issues: software bugs, user account lockouts, contact record updates, service outages, and similar incidents. They are not equipped to address business process questions or technical implementation consulting — their scope is product support. All interactions are managed through the formal case management process via SAP for Me.

Engagement Phase Throughout the entire product lifecycle — implementation, operations, and upgrade phases.

Key Deliverables Support case management, root cause analysis, technical guidance.

Customer Interaction Model Customers submit support cases via their S-user credentials on the SAP for Me portal.

7. Preferred Success — Billable

Scope of Responsibility A personalized, proactive value-added support offering designed for cloud customers. It accelerates time-to-value on cloud investments through proactive engagement, insight-driven recommendations, release guidance, targeted learning plans, and enhanced SLAs.

Engagement Phase Throughout the cloud solution lifecycle (post-go-live, ongoing operations).

Key Deliverables

  • Solution launch reviews and success checks
  • Adoption plans, quality reviews, and business case analysis
  • In-depth release guidance
  • Customized release plans to accelerate readiness and feature adoption
  • Targeted learning plans
  • Key user and administrator training
  • Enhanced support with elevated SLAs
  • Faster incident response and resolution of non-defect issues
  • Dedicated Customer Success experts and SAP subject matter expert access for complex topics

Customer Interaction Model Proactive partnership model with regular check-ins, expert consultations, and learning plan delivery. Extended tiers provide additional expert guidance, enhanced services, and adoption practice support.

Applicable Products: SAP Ariba, SAP Commerce Cloud, SAP Sales Cloud, SAP Service Cloud, SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Fieldglass, SAP Supply Chain Management, SAP BTP, and others.

8. CAS (SAP Cloud Application Services) — Billable

Scope of Responsibility Provides supplementary operational support and managed service subscriptions, with tailored offerings for specific industries such as utilities. Customers may procure CAS on demand for additional cloud application operations support.

Engagement Phase Operations phase (procured on demand).

Key Deliverables Cloud application operations support, industry-specific operational task execution.

Customer Interaction Model Upon subscription, the CAS team delivers dedicated operational support services to the customer.

9. COE Service (Center of Expertise) — Billable

Scope of Responsibility COE is SAP's capability-building and support collaboration framework for partners and customers. SAP recommends that partners establish a COE, supported by dedicated Support Manager and Support Advisor roles, to enable effective collaboration with SAP Support. The framework encompasses service delivery and quality management, certification and resource governance, and support process integration.

Engagement Phase Throughout the implementation and operations lifecycle.

Key Deliverables Support collaboration analytics dashboards (SAP for Me), case resolution optimization, project risk reduction, customer satisfaction improvement.

Customer Interaction Model Partners engage with SAP Support on behalf of customers through the COE mechanism, utilizing S-user credentials to create and manage support cases.

10. Additional Supporting Roles

Cloud Architect & Advisor (CAA)

Scope of Responsibility Collaborates with the sales team to validate order deliverability and prevent downstream surprises. Responsible for supporting the completion of the Customer Onboarding Form (COF) and facilitating the Sales-to-Delivery (S2D) handoff. Re-engaged for complex change requests to ensure commercial agreements are executable.

Engagement Phase Pre-sales through S2D handoff; re-engaged for complex change requests.

Partner Coaching / Partner Mentoring Services

Scope of Responsibility SAP provides dedicated Coaching (10 sessions of 30 minutes each, over 6 months) and Mentoring (1 hour per week, 13 sessions over 3 months) engagements for partners, covering functional solutions, configuration, technical topics, and project management. Applicable to S/4HANA Cloud, SuccessFactors, Ariba, CX, and other product lines.

Engagement Phase Partner implementation phase.

SAP MaxAttention / ActiveAttention

Scope of Responsibility Premium success services providing planning, assurance, and remote expert support for complex system landscapes, programs, and operations across the full solution lifecycle.

Engagement Phase Full lifecycle coverage, particularly suited to large and complex customer environments.

Role Comparison Summary

Role Engagement Phase Primary Responsibility Organizational Unit Customer Type

Sales / IAEPre-sales through contractSolution selection, contract executionSAP SalesAll customers
CSM / D-CSMFull post-contract lifecycleCustomer relationship management, resource coordination, renewalSAP Customer SuccessCloud subscription customers
Onboarding AdvisorEarly post-contractProduct onboarding, service provisioning, enablementSAP Customer SuccessNew cloud customers
ECS (PL / CDM / TSM)System delivery and operationsSystem build and delivery, operational coordination, technical supportSAP ECSMid-to-large cloud customers (S/4HANA, etc.)
TQM / Launch AdvisorFull lifecycle (on demand)Risk identification, project coaching, organizational enablementSAP ECS / MaxAttentionComplex project customers
SAP SupportFull lifecycleCase management, technical support, patch deliverySAP SupportAll customers
Preferred SuccessFull cloud operations lifecycleProactive support, release guidance, learning plans, enhanced SLAsSAP ServicesCloud subscription customers (optional)
CASOperations phase (on demand)Cloud application operations, industry-specific tasksSAP CASSpecific industry cloud customers (optional)
COE ServiceFull implementation and operations lifecycleSupport collaboration, quality management, capability buildingPartner / Customer internalPartners and large customers (optional)

Customer Lifecycle and Role Collaboration

Lifecycle Stage Lead Role Collaborating Roles Key Activities

Pre-SalesSales / IAECAASolution selection, contract negotiation, COF completion
Contract & KickoffCSM / D-CSMOnboarding Advisor, CAAS2D handoff, Onboarding session scheduling, service provisioning
Implementation & Go-LiveECS (PL / CDM / TSM)Onboarding Advisor, Launch Advisor, SAP SupportSystem build and delivery, technical support, risk management
Operations & StabilizationCDM / TSM / SAP SupportPreferred Success, CAS, COEDay-to-day operations, case management, upgrades, continuous improvement
Renewal & ExpansionCSM / E-CSPSales / IAE, Preferred SuccessRenewal negotiation, subscription expansion, value review

Key Clarifications and Considerations

Role Boundaries The Onboarding Advisor's mandate is limited to the onboarding phase. Issues arising during implementation or post-go-live are escalated via the CSM to the appropriate resources, such as implementation consultants — Onboarding Advisors do not maintain ongoing ownership of these matters. Business process and technical implementation consulting should be directed to implementation partners (e.g., EY, IBM, Deloitte) or SAP's own COE delivery organization (available as an optional, billable service).

Role Availability Varies by Contract Type Not all roles are present in every engagement. ECS roles (PL, CDM, TSM) are primarily allocated to mid-to-large S/4HANA cloud customers. Launch Advisor and Preferred Success are both optional, purchasable value-added services.

The CSM as the Central Coordination Hub The CSM is the pivotal coordination role across the customer lifecycle. However, given that a single CSM typically manages multiple customer accounts, it is not feasible for them to maintain deep expertise across every product and handle every issue directly. Their primary value lies in orchestrating the right SAP internal specialists for each situation.

Preferred Success — Extended Tier Building upon the base subscription plan, the Extended tier offers additional expert guidance, enhanced service entitlements, and adoption enablement. It is currently available for SAP Ariba, Commerce Cloud, Sales Cloud, Service Cloud, SuccessFactors, Fieldglass, Supply Chain Management, and BTP.

entry.title: SAP客户支持角色详解看我这篇就够
entry.content:
长期以来，在与SAP客户接触的过程中，我发现很多客户，依然不知道在订阅了SAP的云服务以后，究竟要找SAP的哪些人来做哪些事。ToB的业务不像ToC，打开淘宝找客服就行了，SAP人员众多，涉及的模块和专业知识林林总总，十分容易混淆。今天看我这篇，让你清晰了解SAP公司原厂的员工职责划分，更好的利用SAP的资源。

概述

SAP 针对云时代的订阅模式，构建了一套覆盖客户全生命周期的多层次支持体系。从售前的销售团队，到签约后的 Onboarding 顾问、CSM，再到实施运营阶段的 ECS、TQM、SAP Support，以及可选购的增值服务（Preferred Success、CAS 等），各角色分工明确、协同配合，共同保障客户的成功采用与持续价值实现。

云时代的订阅模式对客户支持的周期有更高更细致的要求，从客户下单到上线，再到续签，都需要不同角色的参与与协调。

1. 销售 / 行业客户经理（Industry Account Executive, IAE）- 内含
  • 职责范围 负责确保为客户的业务需求订购正确的解决方案和服务；贯穿客户整个云旅程，是客户在 SAP 的主要联系人。
  • 服务阶段 售前阶段至合同签署全程，以及后续变更请求阶段。
  • 主要交付物 合同签署、解决方案选型建议、变更订单协调。
  • 与客户的互动方式 直接与客户高层及业务决策者沟通，确保商业协议落地；如无 CSM，也可由销售协调发起 Onboarding 流程。
2. CSM / D-CSM（Customer Success Manager, Digtal CSM) - 内含
  • 职责范围 CSM 与其他云公司的职责并无不同，角色的设计初衷是在客户签单以后负责客户的生命周期管理。注意这里和销售的不同，虽然销售也是客户的全周期，但是销售更关注和“钱”有关系的环节，至于产品使用说明，计费方式，最佳实践，协调SAP其他资源等则要统统交给CSM来完成。但是不是所有的客户都会有CSM，只有那些买够一定数额的产品才会分配对应产品的CSM，举个例子（价格仅仅是假设），如果你买了SAP的BTP产品，同时也购买了SAP的Successfactor，但是BTP你是重度用户买了200万欧元，Successfactor你只是最小量购买，只买了10万欧元，那么你作为客户会分配一个BTP的CSM，不会分配Successfactor的CSM。 这里要特别说明的是，你可能会遇到D-CSM，他们一个人要看上百客户，也会起到CSM的作用，不过他们对某类产品的知识和服务深度，不会到达CSM的层级，如果你遇到售后问题，找他们没有错，他们会帮你协调到对应的资源。
  • 服务阶段 合同签署后，贯穿整个订阅生命周期（上线、运营、续签）。
  • 主要交付物 协调 SAP 内部资源、提升客户满意度、协调 Onboarding 及后续实施顾问介入、推动续签。
  • 与客户的互动方式 定期与客户高层沟通；协调 Onboarding 顾问、实施顾问等内部资源；在客户遇到问题时统筹调度各方支持。

注：在部分文档中，CSM 也被称为 E-CSP（Enterprise Cloud Success Partner），S-CSP 则是专注特定产品线的专项 CSP。

3. Onboarding 顾问（Onboarding Advisor / Onboarding Consultant）- 内含
  • 职责范围 隶属于 SAP Customer Service Delivery（CSD）部门，是分散到各产品线的专家，负责讲解相关服务、带领客户开通并入门，保证按期交付服务，提高客户满意度。
  • 服务阶段 合同签署初期（Onboarding 阶段），重点在客户签约早期发挥作用。
  • 主要交付物 产品入门介绍（如何提交工单、如何管理 S 用户、如何注册网上研讨会）；BTP/BDC/AI 相关服务开通讲解（BTP Cockpit、集成套件、数仓、HANA、SAP Build 等，每项约 1~1.5 小时）；确保客户可以登录相关服务进行后续开发和使用。
  • 与客户的互动方式 通过一系列在线会议提供指导和赋能；可由 CSM 协调发起，也可由销售或客户自行发起，Onboarding顾问还会主动联系客户IT经理，形式十分灵活；客户项目实施过程中及上线后的问题，将由 CSM 协调其他部门（如实施顾问）跟进，Onboarding 顾问不再追踪。
4. ECS（Enterprise Cloud Services）- 内含

ECS 组织下设三个专属角色，主要在私有云ERP系统交付和运营阶段发挥作用。这一块儿主要针对买了SAP S/4HANA private cloud的客户，如果你遇到了BTP的问题比如如何进行子账户创建，如何和SAP身份认证系统打通，如何开通SAP分析云等，不要找他们，没用，找SAP AE/CSM/Onboarding顾问。

  • Project Lead（PL，项目负责人）

    • 职责范围 负责确保 SAP 按照销售和合同约定构建并交付系统；参与系统构建阶段，如后续需要变更或扩展，PL 会再次介入。
    • 服务阶段 系统交付/构建阶段（临时性参与）。[1]
    • 主要交付物 系统按约交付，变更请求落地。
    • 与客户的互动方式 在系统构建阶段与客户直接协作。

  • Client Delivery Manager（CDM，客户交付经理）

    • 职责范围 负责已销售和交付系统的"运营"阶段，是该阶段的单一联系人，协调 S/4HANA 设置和运营的各项事务。
    • 服务阶段 系统运营阶段（长期）。
    • 主要交付物 运营协调、问题升级处理。
    • 与客户的互动方式 作为运营阶段的主要联系人，持续与客户沟通。

  • Technical Service Manager（TSM，技术服务经理）

    • 职责范围 经验丰富的 SAP 技术顾问，与 CDM 紧密合作，是所有技术和基础设施相关主题的主要技术联系人。
    • 服务阶段 系统运营阶段（长期）。
    • 主要交付物 技术问题解决、基础设施支持。
    • 与客户的互动方式 处理技术和基础设施相关问题，与 CDM 协同工作。
5. TQM（Technical Quality Manager）/ Launch Advisor（LA）- 付费
  • 职责范围 Launch Advisor（LA）是经验丰富的多面手"黑带"专家，帮助客户规划和导航云旅程的各个阶段，辅导和培训客户及其组织，提前发现问题或风险，并协助解决。
  • 服务阶段 可在流程中的任何时间点介入，持续时间根据需要而定；越早介入效果越好。
  • 主要交付物 风险识别与规避、项目规划辅导、组织能力建设。
  • 与客户的互动方式 按需订购，提供高度个性化的专家陪伴式服务；SAP 经验表明，对于复杂项目，拥有 Launch Advisor 是重要的成功因素。

注：在 SAP 的不同产品线和服务体系中，类似角色也被称为 TQM（Technical Quality Manager），主要见于 MaxAttention/ActiveAttention 高级支持服务中。

6. SAP Support（标准支持）- 内含
  • 职责范围 简单来说，他们只负责处理产品相关的问题比如Bug，用户锁定，客户联系人更改，serivce宕机等，他们无法处理业务或者技术实施的顾问问题，并且方式是通过SAP for Me开工单的形式来解决。
  • 服务阶段 : 贯穿整个产品生命周期（实施、运营、升级阶段）。
  • 主要交付物 问题工单处理、根本原因分析、技术指导。
  • 与客户的互动方式 客户通过 S 用户在 SAP for Me 提交支持案例
7. Preferred Success（SAP 优选成功服务）- 付费
  • 职责范围 面向云客户的个性化、主动式增值支持服务，通过主动合作、洞察驱动的建议、版本指南、有针对性的学习计划和增强 SLA，帮助客户快速实现云投资价值。
  • 服务阶段 贯穿云解决方案的整个生命周期（上线后持续运营）。
  • 主要交付物
    • 解决方案启动检查和成功检查 采用计划、质量审核、业务案例分析
    • 深度版本指南 定制化版本计划，加快版本准备和功能采用
    • 有针对性的学习计划 关键用户和管理员培训
    • 高级支持和增强 SLA 更快的事件响应和非缺陷问题解决
    • 客户成功专家 SAP 主题专家支持，处理复杂议题
  • 与客户的互动方式 主动式合作伙伴关系，定期检查、专家咨询、学习计划推送；扩展版本提供更多专家指导、增强服务和功能采用实践帮助。

适用产品：SAP Ariba、SAP Commerce Cloud、SAP Sales Cloud、SAP Service Cloud、SAP SuccessFactors、SAP Fieldglass、SAP Supply Chain Management、SAP BTP 等。

8. CAS（SAP Cloud Application Services）- 付费
  • 职责范围 提供额外的支持和运营任务订阅服务，专为特定行业（如公用事业）设计，客户可按需订购以获得额外的云应用运营支持。
  • 服务阶段 系统运营阶段（按需订购）。
  • 主要交付物 云应用运营支持、特定行业运营任务。
  • 与客户的互动方式 客户订购后，由 CAS 团队提供专项运营支持服务。
9. COE Service（Center of Expertise，卓越中心服务）- 付费
  • 职责范围 COE 是 SAP 支持体系中面向合作伙伴和客户的能力建设与支持协作机制。SAP 建议合作伙伴建立 COE，通过支持经理（Support Manager）和支持顾问（Support Advisor）角色，实现与 SAP 支持的高效协作，涵盖服务交付与质量管理、认证与资源管理、支持流程集成等。
  • 服务阶段 贯穿项目实施和运营全周期。
  • 主要交付物 支持协作分析仪表盘（SAP for Me）、案例处理优化、项目风险降低、客户满意度提升。
  • 与客户的互动方式 合作伙伴通过 COE 机制代表客户与 SAP 支持协作，使用 S 用户代表客户创建支持案例。
10. 其他支持角色 

  • Cloud Architect & Advisor（CAA，云架构师与顾问）

    • 职责范围 与销售团队协作，确保订单可交付，避免后续意外；负责协助客户填写 Customer Onboarding Form（COF），支持销售到交付（S2D）的交接；对于复杂变更请求，CAA 会再次介入确保商业协议可落地。
    • 服务阶段 售前至 S2D 交接阶段；复杂变更请求时再次介入。

  • 合作伙伴教练服务 / 合作伙伴导师服务

    • 职责范围 SAP 为合作伙伴提供专属教练（10 场 30 分钟远程会议，6 个月内）和导师（每周 1 小时，3 个月共 13 场）服务，涵盖功能解决方案、配置、技术或项目管理问题，适用于 S/4HANA Cloud、SuccessFactors、Ariba、CX 等产品。
    • 服务阶段 合作伙伴项目实施阶段。

  • SAP MaxAttention / ActiveAttention

    • 职责范围 高级成功服务，为复杂系统架构、项目和运营提供规划和保障，提供远程专家支持，覆盖整个解决方案生命周期。
    • 服务阶段 全生命周期（尤其适合大型复杂客户）。

角色对比总览

角色 服务阶段 主要职责 归属部门 适用客户类型

销售 / IAE售前至签约解决方案选型、合同签署SAP Sales所有客户
CSM / D-CSM签约后全生命周期客户关系管理、资源协调、续签SAP Customer Success云订阅客户
Onboarding 顾问签约初期产品入门、服务开通、赋能SAP Customer Success新签云客户
ECS（PL/CDM/TSM）系统交付与运营系统构建交付、运营协调、技术支持SAP ECS中大型云客户（S/4HANA等）
TQM / Launch Advisor全程（按需）风险识别、项目辅导、组织赋能SAP ECS / MaxAttention复杂项目客户
SAP Support全生命周期工单处理、技术支持、补丁交付SAP Support所有客户
Preferred Success云运营全周期主动支持、版本指南、学习计划、增强SLASAP Services云订阅客户（可选购）
CAS运营阶段（按需）云应用运营支持、特定行业任务SAP CAS特定行业云客户（可选购）
COE Service实施与运营全周期支持协作、质量管理、能力建设合作伙伴/客户内部合作伙伴及大型客户（可选购）

客户生命周期与角色协作关系

生命周期阶段 主导角色 协作角色 关键活动

售前阶段销售 / IAECAA（云架构师）解决方案选型、合同谈判、COF 填写
签约/启动阶段CSM / D-CSMOnboarding 顾问、CAA合同交接（S2D）、Onboarding 会议安排、服务开通
实施/上线阶段ECS（PL/CDM/TSM）Onboarding 顾问、Launch Advisor、SAP Support系统构建交付、技术支持、风险管控
运营/稳定阶段CDM / TSM / SAP SupportPreferred Success、CAS、COE日常运营、工单处理、版本升级、持续优化
续约/扩展阶段CSM / E-CSP销售 / IAE、Preferred Success续签谈判、扩展订阅、价值回顾

关键说明与注意事项

  • 角色边界说明 Onboarding 顾问仅负责客户入门阶段，项目实施过程中及上线后的问题由 CSM 协调实施顾问等其他资源跟进，Onboarding 顾问不再追踪。业务或者技术实施咨询，应该交由合作伙伴partner（比如EY，IBM，德勤等）或者SAP原厂COE实施（可选购）。
  • 角色因合同类型而异 并非所有角色都会同时出现。例如，ECS 的 PL/CDM/TSM 主要服务于中大型 S/4HANA 云客户；Launch Advisor 和 Preferred Success 均为可选购的增值服务。
  • CSM 的协调核心地位 CSM是贯穿客户生命周期的核心协调角色，但由于一个 CSM 通常面对多个客户，无法对每个产品的细节和所有问题全权把控，因此需要协调 SAP 内部各专业资源。
  • Preferred Success 扩展版 在基础订阅计划之上提供更多专家指导、增强服务和功能采用实践帮助，目前适用于 SAP Ariba、Commerce Cloud、Sales Cloud、Service Cloud、SuccessFactors、Fieldglass、Supply Chain Management 和 BTP 等产品。

entry.title: Re: Clarification on data monitor tasks when running cons...
entry.content:
Hi Vasanth,

Your second question appears cut off at "Apart from defining and assigning a new process period cyc…" — could you share the rest so I don't miss any specific configuration requirements?

In short: No, you do not need to run the tasks in the Data Monitor for each Fiscal Year Period (1 through 11) individually. But a brief mechanism clarification is needed first, as it drives the correct setup.

Data Storage in ACDOCU

In Group Reporting, the ACDOCU table stores data periodically (per fiscal period), not Year-To-Date (YTD). YTD figures are produced at reporting time by aggregating the periodic records; it is not how the data is physically stored.

Because of this, a standard Release Universal Journal run in current-period mode for Period 12 releases only Period 12 movements, not the full-year balance. Therefore, a single Period 12 run would leave Periods 1–11 out of ACDOCU, and your yearly consolidation would be incomplete.

The Solution: Prior Periods Mode

The good news for your scenario is that you do not need to open and process the Data Monitor period-by-period. See documentation Prior Periods 

Since release 2502 (and therefore available in your current 2602 release), the Release Universal Journal task offers a "Prior Periods" mode. This mode processes accounting data from closed prior periods in a single run, allowing you to release the whole year at year-end without reopening each interim period.

According to SAP documentation, this feature "eliminates the need to reopen and reprocess the Release Universal Journal task in the current period mode, as well as all subsequent consolidation tasks in respective periods." You can run it on-demand in the Data Monitor or schedule it via the Schedule Jobs for Consolidation Tasks app.

One detail on the run itself: in the Release of Universal Documents screen, you choose between Complete Data and Delta, for either Current Period or Prior Periods. For a greenfield, once-a-year first release, use Complete Data (Delta only transfers changes since a previous release, of which there is none yet). Prior Periods covers the periods before the current one, so period 12 needs a separate current-period run.

Practical Execution for Yearly-Only Consolidation

To execute this correctly:

  1. Set Global Parameters: Set to the final period of the year (e.g., FY 2026, Period 12).
  2. Release Universal Journal — run 1: Prior Periods – Complete Data, selecting periods 1 to 11.
  3. Release Universal Journal — run 2: Current Period – Complete Data, for period 12.
  4. Execute Remaining Tasks: Run the remaining Data Monitor tasks, and then the Consolidation Monitor once for the consolidation period.

  • Note: The consolidation cadence is a separate axis from data collection. Eliminations and other consolidation tasks run once, but only after all periods' reported data are successfully in ACDOCU.

Group Data Analysis - to verify figures, drill down to document level; switch the period selection / periodic vs. cumulative view to confirm every period 1–12 carries the expected values for the unit. See Group Data Analysis 

Quick test: post data across periods 1–11 and 12 for one integrated unit, run Prior Periods – Complete Data (1–11) and then Current Period – Complete Data (12), and confirm via Group Data Analysis that each period carries values and the YTD view is complete.

Hope it helps,

Best Regards,

Michal

entry.title: Issue in execution Allocation Data action
entry.content:
Hi Team,

entry.title: New POD 2.0 Framework: Certificate Check
entry.content:
This blog post is part of a series on SAP Digital Manufacturing new POD 2.0 framework, the new generation of SAP Digital Manufacturing worker UIs.

New POD 2.0 Framework Blogs

  1. The New Generation of SAP Digital Manufacturing Worker UIs
  2. Template-Based Design Instead of POD Types
  3. Visual WYSIWYG Designer
  4. POD 2.0 Hands-on, Flexible Layout Capabilities, Part 1
  5. POD 2.0 Hands-on, Flexible Layout Capabilities, Part 2
  6. Custom UI Dialogs and Calling Production Processes
  7. Dynamic PODs, How to Dynamically Show, Hide, Enable, and Disable UI Elements
  8. Other Business Capabilities Enabled by POD 2.0
  9. Interactive Value Journey (IVJ)
  10. POD Notifications
  11. Tips and Tricks
  12. Accessing PODs 2.0
  13. Implementing Data Scan for Barcode-enabled Component Consumption
  14. Electronic Batch Records (EBR)
  15. Certificate Check in POD 2.0

Introduction

In manufacturing environments, ensuring that only properly qualified personnel perform specific production activities is critical for maintaining product quality, regulatory compliance, operational safety, and workforce accountability. Organizations often need to verify that operators possess the required certifications before allowing them to execute tasks on the shop floor, particularly for specialized equipment, regulated processes, or safety-sensitive operations.

SAP Digital Manufacturing provides built-in Certification Management capabilities that can be associated with manufacturing objects such as Operations, Work Centers, and Resources. Combined with the flexibility of POD 2.0 and Production Processes, these capabilities can be used to automatically validate worker qualifications during production execution and prevent unauthorized activities from being started.

In this blog post, we will explore how to implement a certification validation mechanism in SAP Digital Manufacturing using a Production Process and POD 2.0 actions. By the end, you will have a configurable solution that checks operator certifications before a phase is started and displays a meaningful error message whenever the required qualifications are not assigned to the worker.

Setup

First, create new Certifications in Manage Certifications app, and assign to the Manufacturing Objects like an Operation Activity, Work Center and/or Resource, etc, where these Certifications are required to be assigned to individual workers, before they can start any manufacturing activities.

For this example, I create the two Certifications below:

Next, assign Certifications to Workers via Manage User Assignments app. In this example, I assigned only one of the Certifications to my own user.

Then, create a Production Process to perform the Certification Check using Production Process Design app.

The Production Process should have Input Parameters including: inMaterial, inOperation, inPlant, inResource, inUser and inWorkCenter, all of type String and marked as required.

On the left hand side, under ‘Services and Processes’, drag and drop ‘Certification Check’ service to the design canvas and map the INPUT parameters as follow:

Add a Condition control, and define (1) as 'Service_Certification_Check#isCertificationForbidden' == true:

Click '...' on the top-right corner, and select 'Manage Error Codes':

And create an Error Code like:

Add an Error End control,  select the newly created Error Code, and link the Condition output (1) to it. Link the Else (2) to the End control.

Finally, click "..." for the Cloud Process, select 'Edit Header', and enable 'Publish to Service Registry', so this Cloud Process can be triggered from the POD 2.0.

Save and deploy it.

Next, open Manage PODs 2.0 app and open / create a POD. In this example, I´m using an Order POD.

Select the PhaseList plugin and click on 'Edit Actions' under 'Start Phase' on the right-hand side Properties panel:

Click '+' to add a new Action, select 'ProductionProcess', map the newly created Production Process, link the Input Parameters to Variables from POD Context and make sure you use the Arrow Up button to move the Production Process up, before the StartPhase action, so it runs before the phase start and can prevent it in case of failure:

With all this setup in place, you should be ready to test it.

Run

To give it a try, click Run (Play button) in Manage PODs 2.0 app to load the POD, and then filter, select and open a order where the master data matches the assignment of the Certifications created in Manage Certifications app, and click 'Start'.

If the User has the Certification assigned, it should work just fine and the phase should be started:

However, if the Certification is not assigned, the Production Process should fail, fall on the Error End, and display the Error Code message defined:

Then, as soon as the missing Certification is assigned to the User in Manage User Assignments app:

The Phase Start should work just fine as well:

Conclusion

By combining SAP Digital Manufacturing Certifications, Production Processes, and POD 2.0 actions, manufacturers can implement robust certification controls directly within production execution workflows. This approach ensures that only appropriately certified workers can perform specific manufacturing activities, helping organizations improve compliance, reduce operational risk, and maintain consistent process quality.

As demonstrated in this example, the Certification Check service can be seamlessly integrated into the phase start process, allowing qualification validation to occur automatically and in real time. When certification requirements are not met, execution can be prevented immediately with clear user feedback, while certified operators can proceed without additional manual verification steps.

This pattern can be extended to a wide range of business scenarios, including safety-critical operations, regulated industries, specialized equipment usage, and workforce qualification management.

Leveraging the extensibility of POD 2.0 and Production Processes enables organizations to build powerful governance mechanisms while maintaining a streamlined and user-friendly operator experience.

 

Do you like this post? Please let me know in the comments section what you think. Any feedback is highly appreciated.
Or, if you have any questions, please check SAP Community Q&A Area, or comment down below.

Thanks,
Manoel Costa

entry.title: Freight Order Execution Monitoring
entry.content:
Freight Order Execution Monitoring app gives KPI based view of Freight Orders, so they do not need to open each document manually. From the monitoring screen, users can drill down into detailed execution status and take action where needed.

The app helps Dispatcher users to:

  • Monitor Freight Orders by execution status
  • Identify delayed or incomplete Freight Orders
  • Compare planned and actual execution updates
  • Review missing arrival or departure events
  • Support smoother settlement and reporting

This app is very useful for daily transportation operations. If execution events are missed, it can create issues later in charge calculation, settlement and tracking carrier payment. By using this app regularly, teams can catch execution gaps early and keep the Freight Order process transparent.

Activity On Time Performance:


Carrier On Time Performance:

Conclusion
The Freight Order Execution Monitoring app gives better visibility and control over the Freight Order lifecycle. It helps operations teams focus on exceptions, improve follow up, and ensure execution updates are completed before settlement processes begin.

Reference:

entry.title: Manyat Layout: Shifting Inventory Readiness Ahead of Wave Execution in SAP EWM
entry.content:
Manyat Layout: Shifting Inventory Readiness Ahead of Wave Execution in SAP EWM

 

Introduction

While studying SAP EWM Wave Management, I kept asking myself a simple operational question:

Why do we wait for Wave execution before preparing inventory?

In many warehouse environments, outbound demand is already visible before execution begins. Sales orders may already exist, shipping schedules may already be known, and expected inventory requirements may already be identified.

This raises an interesting question:

If tomorrow's outbound demand is already known today, can part of the inventory preparation begin before Wave execution starts?

This question became the foundation of what I call the Manyat Layout Concept.

The objective of this article is not to replace SAP EWM functionality. Instead, it explores whether physical inventory preparation can begin earlier when outbound demand is already known.

Standard SAP EWM Wave Process

In SAP EWM, Wave Management is configured through standard Wave Management functionality.

Common transactions and areas associated with Wave processing include:

  • /SCWM/WAVE

  • /SCWM/WAVETMP

  • Wave Templates

  • Wave Release Processing

  • Warehouse Task Creation

  • Warehouse Order Creation Rules (WOCR)

A traditional outbound process often follows this sequence:

Demand → Wave Creation → Warehouse Tasks → WOCR → Warehouse Orders → Picking → Goods Issue

This process is proven and widely used across warehouse operations.

Most optimization activities begin after the Wave is released.

Warehouse Tasks are generated.

WOCR organizes the work.

Warehouse Orders are created.

Resources begin execution.

The Manyat Layout Concept

Manyat Layout introduces an additional preparation stage before execution:

Possible Preparation Before Standard Wave Execution

Demand → Material Identification → Replenishment → Readiness Zone

Standard SAP EWM Flow Continues

Wave Release → Warehouse Tasks → WOCR → Warehouse Orders → Picking → Goods Issue

The philosophy behind the concept is simple:

Prepare First. Execute Later.

The objective is not to replace SAP EWM functionality.

The objective is to explore whether inventory associated with expected outbound demand can be prepared before execution activities begin.

If inventory is already positioned closer to outbound operations, the warehouse may begin execution from a more prepared state.

What Is the Readiness Zone?

The Readiness Zone is not a traditional storage area.

It is not intended to become a buffer stock location.

Instead, it represents a preparation area where inventory associated with known outbound demand can be positioned before execution begins.

Only materials linked to expected demand are moved.

The concept remains demand-driven rather than storage-driven.

The objective is operational readiness rather than inventory accumulation.

What About WOCR?

A common question is:

If WOCR already organizes work, why is this concept needed?

WOCR remains an important SAP EWM functionality.

The concept does not replace WOCR.

The concept does not remove WOCR.

Manyat Layout does not ask how to replace WOCR.

It asks whether part of the preparation effort can happen before WOCR starts working.

If inventory is already positioned closer to outbound execution:

  • Picker travel distance may decrease.

  • Search activities may be reduced.

  • Warehouse preparation may improve.

  • Execution may become faster.

WOCR can still perform grouping and optimization activities according to demand and warehouse requirements.

The goal is not to replace SAP logic.

The goal is to reduce unnecessary movement before execution begins.

Another Perspective: Execution-Stage Dependencies

Another perspective involves execution-stage dependencies.

In many warehouse environments, execution activities begin only after multiple planning and grouping steps have been completed.

If an issue occurs during these stages, warehouse execution may be delayed while corrective actions are performed.

When inventory has already been prepared and positioned in a Readiness Zone, the warehouse enters execution from a more prepared state.

The concept does not eliminate system issues.

However, it raises an interesting operational question:

Can early inventory preparation reduce the operational impact of execution-stage disruptions by improving warehouse readiness before execution begins?

This article does not claim a definitive answer.

Instead, it explores whether preparation itself can become an additional layer of operational readiness before standard SAP EWM execution processes begin.

A Real-World Example

Consider a warehouse that already knows the next morning's outbound requirements.

Under a traditional approach, inventory remains in its original storage locations until execution begins.

Under the Manyat Layout approach:

  • Required inventory is identified early.

  • Replenishment moves inventory into a Readiness Zone.

  • Inventory is prepared before execution starts.

  • Wave processing and WOCR continue as standard SAP EWM activities.

When execution begins, the warehouse starts from a prepared state rather than a waiting state.

Where AI Can Help

Artificial Intelligence introduces additional possibilities.

AI can support this concept by:

  • Analyzing historical demand patterns.

  • Identifying fast-moving materials.

  • Predicting future outbound demand.

  • Supporting replenishment decisions.

  • Recommending Readiness Zone preparation.

As forecasting becomes more accurate, warehouse preparation may become increasingly proactive rather than reactive.

The Real Question

This article does not attempt to replace SAP EWM standard functionality.

Instead, it raises a practical operational question:

If outbound demand is already known, can inventory preparation begin before Wave execution starts?

And if preparation begins earlier:

Can warehouse readiness improve before execution activities even begin?

Conclusion

Traditional warehouse optimization often focuses on execution.

Manyat Layout focuses on preparation.

The concept suggests that some of the most valuable warehouse improvements may occur before the first picking activity begins.

Fast picking creates activity.

Prepared picking creates efficiency.

And sometimes the biggest warehouse optimization happens before the first pick starts.

— Rahul Kumar

Manyat Thinking

X-Raying Warehouse Operations Through SAP EWM

entry.title: Re: BO 4.3 SP05 upgrade failing because of SQL Anywhere logs
entry.content:
With the help of SAP Support, we discovered a very strange thing.

In the setupengine.log file, about 30 minutes after the start of the upgrade, there is this entry:

18:50:30.540 RunCommand: Get CmdLine: E:\SAPBusinessObjects\sqlanywhere\bin64\dbsvc -t Network -s Automatic -as -i -sn "SQL Anywhere for SAP Business Intelligence" -sd "Provides the CMS repository and auditing database for SAP Business Intelligence" -y -w "SQLAnywhereForBI" "E:\SAPBusinessObjects\sqlanywhere\bin64\dbsrv17.exe" -n "BI4" -x tcpip(PORT=2638;DoBroadcast=NO;BroadcastListener=NO) "E:\SAPBusinessObjects\sqlanywhere\database\BI4_CMS.db" "E:\SAPBusinessObjects\sqlanywhere\database\BI4_Audit.db"

This is strange and incorrect, since the database files used by this system are in another path, on disk G: !!!

This could be the cause of the error that causes the upgrade to fail, however, I wonder, why does the installer try to register the SQL Anywhere service by looking for the database files in an incorrect path?
Is there a way to fix it or have I to find a work around (i.e. a sym link ...)

Thank you all.

Thank you in advance for your guidance. Kind regards,

entry.title: Re: Material Description missing in Compare Supplier Quot...
entry.content:
Dear @khaing63,

In Public Cloud the material description cannot be enabled in the standard Compare Supplier Quotations app by using Adapt UI. This is a standard app limitation, not an authorization issue. SAP confirms for the Compare Supplier Quotations app that additional fields cannot be added. The app uses backend service MM_PUR_QTN_COMPARE_SRV and the CDS views C_RequestForQuotationCompare and C_SupplierQuotationCompare. These CDS views do not support metadata extension, therefore the app does not provide extensibility for adding further fields such as material description

The standard Compare Supplier Quotations app should therefore be used for the delivered quotation comparison and awarding process only. If the material description is required, it needs to be checked in the related RFQ or supplier quotation item details, for example via Manage RFQs / Manage Supplier Quotations, depending on the process used

If the business requires a side-by-side comparison including material description, the clean-core approach would be to create a separate custom analytical/list report or side-by-side extension based on released procurement data sources/APIs. The standard Compare Supplier Quotations app itself should not be positioned as extensible for this requirement. In parallel, if this requirement is important for the standard product, it can be submitted as an improvement request via SAP Influence where customers and partners can submit and vote on non-go-live-critical improvement requests

More details below:

  • SAP KBA 3411976 – Extend the app Compare Supplier Quotations
  • SAP Help Compare Supplier Quotations
  • SAP Influence

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Warehouse Task Concept in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition – Mapping from SAP Business ByDesign Inte
entry.content:
We are currently working with a customer who uses a .NET-based solution integrated with SAP Business ByDesign.

In the current ByD setup, warehouse operators work based on logistics tasks generated by the system (e.g., Goods Receipt, Stock Transfer, Picking). Our WMS consumes these tasks and allows operators to execute and confirm them. This process is supported through APIs such as SiteLogisticsTask and WarehouseConfirmation.

 

The customer is now evaluating a transition to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and would like to maintain the same operational model.

 

While we understand that S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition provides Warehouse Management capabilities and supports API-based integration with external systems, we are struggling to identify the equivalent of the ByD logistics task concept.

 

Our questions are:

  • Is there an equivalent to ByD Logistics Tasks / Warehouse Tasks in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?
  • Can these tasks be created, retrieved, processed, and confirmed through standard APIs by an external WMS?
  • Which business objects and APIs should be considered as the equivalent of SiteLogisticsTask and WarehouseConfirmation in ByD?
  • Are there any limitations or recommended approaches when implementing a task-driven warehouse execution process with an external WMS in Public Cloud?

Our objective is to offer the customer the same warehouse execution experience they currently have in ByD while following SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition best practices and Clean Core principles.

Any guidance, architecture recommendations, or references to relevant APIs and business objects would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: AI-Powered Customer Experience in the Travel and Transportation Industry
entry.content:
Delivering Seamless, Personalized Journeys

When we talk about Travel and Transportation there is only one thing a business should focus on, and that is ‘end-to-end’ customer journey. Excellence in experience is what you strive for from the very first point of interaction to the destination. Travelers nowadays have very strict requirements, from booking a ticket to arriving at a destination, they expect fast, convenient, and prompt assistance when they face issues along the way. No matter how perfectly we design a system to meet the needs, there will always be situations where we can’t avoid. Delays, confusing booking systems, long customer service wait times and many more creates frustration for travelers.

AI (Artificial Intelligence) is reshaping how Travel and Transportation industry is doing business. It delivers more avenues to provide customer care aside from the typical communication channels such as email, short messaging services, and social media. AI provides smarter, faster and more personalized customer experiences leading to happier customers which entails to growth in revenue for the business. AI is no longer optional, it is now becoming a necessity – The question is, HOW?

Beyond Tickets and Timetables: How AI Orchestrates the Customer Journey

Previously, travelers preferred travel agents over booking apps, relied on printed tickets, and valued personal service and human interaction. However, mobile apps for bookings, check-ins, and payments are now widely used. Travelers also expect real-time updates and personalized recommendations that provide seamless, end-to-end experiences.

SAP delivers an ecosystem that provides all the tools needed to meet these expectations. Using Service Cloud Version 2, organizations can manage cases, complaints, and information requests efficiently. AI can respond within seconds, unlike traditional customer service processes that rely on manual handling of support tickets. AI-powered chatbots can also manage a high volume of customer conversations simultaneously.

Here is an example integration strategy,

Data Type

Target Systems

Description

Customer Profile

Sales, Service, Commerce and Marketing

Implement for centralize account management, B2B/B2C Partnerships and real time account updates

Booking

Commerce, Service, and Marketing

Leverage AI driven product recommendations to automatically suggest hotel accommodations, flight and transportation combinations

Browsing Behavior

Commerce, Sales, and Marketing

Create dynamic customer segments based on travel preferences, booking patterns and engagement history

Loyalty Status

SAP Loyalty Management Program, Commerce

A centralized loyalty platform enables businesses to manage diverse loyalty programs, rewards, tiers, and promotions across multiple brands and regions from a single system while maintaining local flexibility and control

Support Interactions

Service

Provides unified customer support across chat, email and social media

AI

Sales, Service, and Commerce

Leverage AI to provide contextual travel recommendations based on customer preferences

Commerce Cloud Intelligent Selling Services

Intelligent Selling Services (ISS) for SAP Commerce Cloud is an AI-powered enhancement that leverages machine learning and artificial intelligence to deliver personalized customer experiences and optimize booking strategies. This help in analyzing customer booking pattern and provide contextual data across customer touchpoint and can provide recommendations which can lead increase in revenue.

Commerce Cloud Travel Accelerator

This industry-specific solution is designed to help travel companies deliver omnichannel digital traveler engagement through SAP Commerce Cloud. Organizations that already have an existing SAP Commerce Cloud solution can leverage this add-on to tailor the platform to their unique business requirements. By utilizing this solution, companies can access real-time information to deliver personalized customer experiences, strengthen customer loyalty, and drive greater business value.

Loyalty Management Program through Integration

Of course, a customer will need a system to reward customers for coming back. it’s like earning points, perks, or special treatment when you repeatedly book with the same travel company, for that an integration to loyalty management program either our very own solution or a 3rd party solution can be used.

The Way Forward

With the Advanced Success Plan, we can help you achieve your business goals. As a starting point, we will create a service engagement plan that provides a tailored approach to meeting your KPIs. During this phase, we will also deliver sessions to help you set up Sales Cloud, Service Cloud, and Commerce Cloud while ensuring that Travel and Transportation industry best practices are followed.

With AI capabilities available across every solution, you can now categorize your customer base based on travel behaviors and patterns, as well as perform sentiment analysis on customer reviews and support tickets. The SAP Advanced Success Plan can serve as a strategic partner in helping you achieve your AI objectives. Our experts, backed by deep industry knowledge, can provide guidance on the most effective path forward.

To deliver services that are aligned with each customer's specific goals, we have organized our offerings into four phases: implementation, pre-go-live, post-go-live, and continuous improvement.

Implementation:

During the implementation phase, our focus is on providing adoption guidance to help you set up your solutions, from front-end applications to back-end systems. We work alongside your team to establish the core capabilities needed for a successful implementation and to ensure the solution is aligned with your business requirements.

This includes, but is not limited to, application user management, key user extensibility, the CX AI Toolkit, integrations, security considerations, and other essential platform capabilities. Our goal is to help you build a solid foundation that supports scalability, maintainability, and future growth while enabling your teams to get the most value from the platform.

Pre-Go Live:

In the pre-go-live phase, our focus is to validate and safeguard the solutions that have been built throughout the implementation. The goal is to make sure your systems are configured correctly, performing as expected, and ready when you go live.

This includes conducting detailed reviews of business configuration settings, evaluating system performance, validating integrations, reviewing security and user access setups, and assessing analytics and reporting capabilities. We also help identify potential risks, gaps, or areas for optimization before launch, ensuring that any issues are addressed proactively rather than after go-live.

In addition, we work with your teams to confirm readiness across key functional and technical areas, helping ensure that testing has been completed successfully, critical business scenarios have been validated, and the solution is aligned with your operational requirements. By performing an Adoption Checkpoint during this phase, we help reduce risk, improve system stability, and support a smoother go-live experience.

Post-Go Live:

During the post-go-live phase, we work closely with your team to ensure that recommendations and best practices identified throughout the implementation have been properly configured and are delivering the intended results.

As your users begin working in the production environment, new questions, opportunities for optimization, and minor challenges often emerge. During this stage, we provide continued guidance and support to help address those items, whether they are related to business processes, system configuration, integrations, extensibility, analytics, or overall solution adoption.

Our functional and technical experts remain available to review issues, provide recommendations, and help your team navigate any areas that require additional attention. We also help identify opportunities for further improvements and knowledge transfer, ensuring your organization is well-positioned to maintain, enhance, and scale the solution over time.

Continuous Improvement:

Finally, as part of the continuous improvement phase, we help ensure that you remain informed about new innovations and enhancements introduced through SAP release cycles. By staying up to date with the latest capabilities, you can continue to maximize the value of your solutions and drive ongoing business success.

entry.title: WMS API Equivalents in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition vs. SAP Business ByDesign
entry.content:
We are currently working with a customer who uses a .NET-based solution integrated with SAP Business ByDesign.

In the current ByD setup, warehouse operators work based on logistics tasks generated by the system (e.g., Goods Receipt, Stock Transfer, Picking). Our WMS consumes these tasks and allows operators to execute and confirm them. This process is supported through APIs such as SiteLogisticsTask and WarehouseConfirmation.

The customer is now evaluating a transition to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and would like to maintain the same operational model.

 

While we understand that S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition provides Warehouse Management capabilities and supports API-based integration with external systems, we are struggling to identify the equivalent of the ByD logistics task concept.

 

Our questions are:

  • Is there an equivalent to ByD Logistics Tasks / Warehouse Tasks in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?
  • Can these tasks be created, retrieved, processed, and confirmed through standard APIs by an external WMS?
  • Which business objects and APIs should be considered as the equivalent of SiteLogisticsTask and WarehouseConfirmation in ByD?
  • Are there any limitations or recommended approaches when implementing a task-driven warehouse execution process with an external WMS in Public Cloud?

Our objective is to offer the customer the same warehouse execution experience they currently have in ByD while following SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition best practices and Clean Core principles.

Any guidance, architecture recommendations, or references to relevant APIs and business objects would be greatly appreciated.
