entry.title: The living loop: Circular Manufacturing Framework with Nesting Intelligence - Assisted by AI
entry.content:
The core problem

Conventional scrap reduction fails because it treats symptoms, not the system. Three structural blind spots drive most losses: 3PL warehouses operating without real-time visibility or remnant data; nesting optimized per job rather than across the full material lifecycle; and SAP's Material Ledger doesn't the full information, leaving variances unattributed and finance unable to trace losses to root causes.

The achieved result - Citing a business use case.

At a major aerospace OEM, the Manufacturing Loop reduced titanium scrap from 26% to 21% across 13,000 production orders and $900M of managed inventory, delivering a verified $5M bottom-line saving within 12 months.

  

Annual titanium spends

$448M

Scrap pre-program

$116.5M ($448M × 26%)

Scrap post-program

$94.1M ($448M × 21%)

Gross recovery potential

$22.4M

Verified net saving (Phase 1)

$5M (after ~$17.4M program costs)

The four implementation levers

  1. Nesting optimization — Cross-order, cross-inventory nesting that queries SAP in real time, prioritizes remnants, and flags additive manufacturing where it is the lower-cost route. The primary driver of the 5-point reduction.
  2. 3PL integration — Connected $900M of previously invisible inventory to SAP procurement logic, stopping duplicate purchasing against stock already paid for, including taking effect of the remnants in stock. 
  3. Additive manufacturing — Introduced for highest-scrap geometries as a material cost decision. Buy-to-fly ratio: 8:1–16:1 conventional vs 1.5:1–2:1 additive.
  4. SAP Material Ledger & product costing — Activated actual costing with full information, to create attributable variance lines (scrap, yield, purchase price, production), making the $5M visible and defensible.

Phased path to 60–70% scrap reduction

Phase

Scrap

Saving

Basis

Phase 1 — Achieved

26% → 21%

$5M

Verified

Phase 2 — Projected

21% → 14–15%

~$10M

Additive at scale + remnant automation

Phase 3 — Projected

14–15% → 8–10%

~$8M

Closed-loop governance + SAP actual costing

Cumulative

26% → 8–10%

~$23M

62–69% reduction — within 60–70% range

    

The framework: four layers

  • Diagnose — Waste mapping, root cause analysis, value stream mapping
  • Activate — Nesting engine drawing from live SAP inventory before triggering procurement
  • Build — Dual sourcing, connected 3PL, predictive replenishment
  • Sustain — SAP Material Ledger delivering actual cost visibility across all variance categories

Governance

Daily OEE reviews · Weekly scrap reviews · Monthly Material Ledger variance analysis · Quarterly 3PL and supplier audits · Statistical process control · Digital part traceability back to sheet, nest, and production order.

The return

Outcome

Figure

Basis

Scrap rate reduction

60–70%

Phase 1 verified at 19.2%; full program 62–69% cumulative

OEE improvement

10–15 pts

Aerospace baseline 55–70%; 5–10 pts per phase 

Annual recoverable value

$10–20M

Modelled from $448M spend at program maturity; Phase 1 verified at $5M

The Manufacturing Loop underlines the fact that scrap is not an unavoidable cost of precision manufacturing — it is a diagnostic signal pointing directly to isolated systems, unscored remnants, and financial blind spots that have persisted simply because no one has built the bridge between the nesting engine and the SAP Material Ledger. By connecting those two worlds in real time, manufacturers managing high-value materials like titanium can recover millions in previously invisible losses — as verified through a $5M saving achieved at a major Tier 1 aerospace OEM within 12 months.

If you are working in a manufacturing environment where scrap variance is visible on your P&L but untraceable to a root cause, start with one question: does your nesting engine know what SAP knows right now? The answer will tell you exactly where your loop is broken — and where to close it first.

I would love to hear from experts and practitioners in the field — whether you have faced similar integration challenges, implemented parts of this framework, or had questions about how the SAP Material Ledger fits into your specific environment. Please share your thoughts, experiences, or questions in the comments below. Every conversation makes the loop a little tighter.

entry.title: Re: Cloud Connector 2.19.0.2 is here!
entry.content:
Hi Devin,

the fact that Tomcat has a security vulnerability does not automatically mean, that a Cloud Connector installation is affected by this. Cloud Connector has embedded Tomcat libraries adapted to its needs and only uses a small portion of Tomcat features. Furthermore, Cloud Connector is not meant to act as a public web server, which means that even if a Tomcat vulnerability would be relevant, then this usually would only be exploitable from within the own intranet, but not from cloud side or from the internet.

I can tell you that security issues of all used Open Source components - which of course also includes Tomcat - are actively monitored and assessed by responsible SAP teams, and appropriate security patches will be published asap, if required.

However, what is also part of the security principles, is that we do not discuss security vulnerabilities or disclose any related info in a public forum like this. So, if you have security concerns, and would like to discuss them, please create an SAP support case for this.
Thank you for your understanding.

entry.title: New Course Extending SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2 using In-App Extensibility
entry.content:
Walking towards the summer (or winter depending where you are) with you SAP CX Community! Fernando is here, checking in and kicking off a new SAP CX Integration and Extensibility blog post.

From last year onwards, our learning production focus is on SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 2 extensibility and integration topics. Today, I'm delighted to announce in this blog post the next major learning milestone for those looking to master the InApp extensibility of SAP Sales and Service Cloud version 2.

So, please, join me in welcoming our newest addition to the SAP's learning platform: 


New Course Extending SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2 using In-App Extensibility

If you're an adminsitrator,  business consultant, architect, or developer looking to customize and enhance SAP Sales and Service Cloud version 2 using built‑in tools, you've come to the right place. Here, you'll gain the practical knowledge needed to implement your custom business requirements.

We've structured our learning content to ensure that, after finishing this course participants will have the skills to create and modify custom fields, implement business rules, adapt UI elements and interact with external systems within SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 2.

Here's a glimpse of what it looks like:

 


Audience
The course is meant to be helpful for:

  • Administrators
  • Business Users
  • Developers
  • Technical Consultants
  • Architects

Learning Objectives
This course teaches how to customize and enhance SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2 using built‑in tools without deep programming.. You’ll follow a practical knowledge covering personalization and adaptation of the UI, extending business entities, implementing custom logic,  and to interact with external systems.

Learning Content
The course consists of 4 units and provides more than 2 hours of content:

  • Introducing In-App Extensibility in SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2
    Presents In‑app extensibility in SAP Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2 to enable businesses to quickly and safely tailor applications through intuitive UI tools delivering flexible, upgrade‑proof enhancements with minimal effort.
  •  Enhancing User Experience with Personalization and Adaptation
    Fine tune user experience in SAP Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2 through personalization and adaptation capabilities that let users tailor interfaces, configure behavior, and assign dynamic page layouts to match individual roles and business needs.
  • Extending Business Entities
    Customizing business entities in SAP Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2 empowers organizations to enrich standard objects by adding custom fields, implementing tailored business logic, and managing dynamic property rules to support more flexible and intelligent business processes.
  • Interacting with External Systems
    The integration of external systems in SAP Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2 enables seamless integration through interaction types, external hooks, mashups with web applications, and automated workflows, helping streamline processes and extend functionality beyond the core system.

After completing successfully this course you'll be granted with a digital badge like this one which could be shared and linked to your social networks:

Enroll on  it today!  We’re launching an exciting new series of extensibility learning content for SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 2, join us to boost your team’s skills and share feedback to help shape even better lessons.

Additional Resources
You can find a related live session on In-App extensibility for SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud version 2. For additional free learning journeys and courses, visit the SAP Learning site for Integration and Extesibility to build in-demand SAP skills on a variety of topics, for the SAP CX Solutions portfolio. If you enjoy quick video learning, explore our Integration and Extensibility videos for SAP Customer Experience for the newest updates on integration and extensibility for SAP Customer Experience solutions.

entry.title: Unable to Reconcile Vendor Down Payment with AP Invoice Containing Withholding Tax in SAP Business O
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am facing an issue in SAP Business One 10.0 while trying to reconcile a Vendor Down Payment against an AP Invoice.

Scenario:

  1. An AP Invoice was created approximately one month ago.

  2. Later, a Vendor Down Payment was made to the same vendor.

  3. The vendor has adjusted the down payment amount against the AP Invoice.

  4. I am now attempting to reconcile the Vendor Down Payment with the AP Invoice through Internal Reconciliation.

Document Details:

  • AP Invoice Amount: INR 2,915,439.50

  • Withholding Tax (WHT): INR 2,500.00

  • Vendor Down Payment: INR 11,141,570.00

Issue:
When attempting the reconciliation, SAP Business One displays the following error message:

"You can only link to the section of the document which has no withholding tax."

I would like to understand:

  • Is this a standard SAP Business One limitation when an AP Invoice contains Withholding Tax?

  • What is the recommended approach to reconcile a Vendor Down Payment against an AP Invoice that includes WHT?

  • Are there any additional steps or workarounds required to complete the reconciliation?

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Where can I see SXI_MONITOR errors after successful r...
entry.content:
Hi,

The Only Places You May Still Find It
If the event was recent enough, check OS-level traces:

ECC ABAP Instance

Work process traces in:

  • ST11
  • dev traces:
    • dev_w*
    • dev_rfc*
    • dev_icm
    • dev_rd

Look around the exact timestamp.

Typical transient entries:

  • partner not reached
  • HTTPIO_PLG_CANCELED
  • connection reset
  • timeout during receive
  • location CPIC
  • ICM_HTTP_CONNECTION_BROKEN

 

PI/PO Java Side

If using Java Adapter Engine:

In:

  • NWA logs
  • defaultTrace
  • XPI Inspector traces

You may still find:

  • adapter reconnects
  • HTTP 503
  • JMS reconnects
  • temporary channel failures

Rgds

Dino

entry.title: Why is deployment mandatory for a root item type if the documentation says GenericItems is used as a
entry.content:
I'm reading the SAP Commerce documentation regarding item type deployments, which states:

Failing to declare a deployment for a type means that all instances of that type are stored in the genericitems table with all the other types that have no deployment table...

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_COMMERCE_CLOUD_PUBLIC_CLOUD/51e73d14aedc487384e4518b60a1f5fd/8ecae959b9bd46b8b426fa8dbde5cac4.html#:~:text=Failing%20to%20declare%20a%20deployment%20for%20a%20type%20means%20that%20all%20instances%20of%20that%20type%20are%20stored%20in%20the%20genericitems%20table%20with%20all%20the%20other%20types%20that%20have%20no%20deployment%20table. 

Based on this, my understanding was that if a type does not define a deployment, SAP Commerce would fall back to storing instances in the genericitems table, although this is discouraged due to performance implications.

My code

 
<itemtype code="MyCustomType"
          autocreate="true"
          generate="true"
          extends="GenericItem">
    <attributes>
        <attribute qualifier="name" type="java.lang.String">
            <persistence type="property"/>
        </attribute>
    </attributes>
</itemtype>

However, the build fails during validation with the following error:

[ycheckdeployments] No deployment defined for non-abstract itemType MyCustomType in file: .../resources/myextension-items.xml

Could someone clarify how these two behaviors fit together?

  • Is the GenericItems fallback described in the documentation referring to older platform behavior?

Thanks

entry.title: SAP SUM Reset: When, Why, and How to Reset SUM
entry.content:
Hello Reader,

Let's be real — if you're reading this, things probably haven't gone according to plan. Your SUM run is stuck somewhere in the Execution phase, your client wants answers, and the clock is ticking.
You're not alone. This is one of the most common and stressful situations a BASIS administrator can face.

This guide will walk you through everything you need to know — not just the commands, but the logic behind them — so you can make confident decisions under pressure.

Moreover, this would be a Quick Answers to What You're Probably Googling Right Now:

  • Can I reset SUM right now?
  • What happens if I just restore the database?
  • What if I've already deleted the SUM directory?

What Does a SUM Reset Actually Do?

A SUM reset is designed to bring the system back to a state where the upgrade can be restarted.

Depending on the phase and scenario, SUM can:

  • Reverse upgrade activities
  • Remove upgrade artifacts
  • Clean up the shadow instance
  • Restore upgrade consistency

A reset is different from:

  • Stopping SUM
  • Deleting the SUM directory
  • Restoring only the database

When Should You Consider a SUM Reset?

A SUM reset is typically considered in the following situations:

  1. Upgrade Stuck in Execution Phase
    You have spent considerable time analysing logs and retrying phases, but the upgrade is still blocked.
  2. Downtime Window Is Running Out
    Business requirements may force you to abandon the upgrade and return the system to service.
  3. Upgrade State Has Become Inconsistent
    Repeated failures, corrupted upgrade metadata, or unexpected SUM behaviour may require a complete restart.
  4. Customer Requests Rollback
    Sometimes the safest option is to stop the upgrade and plan another attempt later.

When Is a Reset Actually Possible?

This is one of the most important things to understand before you do anything. SUM's ability to reset depends on two conditions:

Condition

Can You Reset?

Notes

Cleanup has NOT been run

Yes

This is the normal rollback path

Cleanup HAS been run (after success)

No

You must use a pre-upgrade system backup

You chose Reset, reset completed

Cleanup is now mandatory

You must run cleanup after a successful reset

SUM directory was deleted manually

Partially

Re-extract SAR file, then follow the stuck-phase procedure

Important: Never run the Cleanup option from SUM's More menu unless you are 100% sure you won't need to roll back. Once cleanup runs, the only way back is a full system restore from a backup taken before the upgrade began.

 

Why Restoring the Database Is Not Enough

One of the most overlooked aspects of SAP upgrades is the shadow instance.

During an upgrade, SUM creates a shadow system that consumes:

  • CPU
  • Memory
  • Disk
  • SAP resources

If you only restore the database:

✓ Database returns to its previous state

X Shadow instance may still exist

X Upgrade artifacts may remain

X Future upgrades may encounter issues

This is why a proper SUM reset is often necessary after restoring the database.

 

Best Practice Before Entering Execution Phase

Before any major upgrade activity begins, make sure you have:

Full Database Backup

This is your primary recovery mechanism.

Backup of the SUM Directory

I strongly recommend taking a backup of the complete SUM directory at the end of preprocessing.

If a rollback becomes necessary later, this backup can save a significant amount of time.

 

How to Reset SUM Correctly

Scenario 1: SUM Reset Option Is Available

This is the easiest situation.

From the SUM UI: More → Reset

Allow SUM to complete the reset process.

After completion:

  • Upgrade changes are reversed
  • Shadow instance is removed
  • Upgrade state is cleaned up

Scenario 2: Upgrade Is Already Deep Into Execution

If rollback is required:

Step 1: Restore and recover the database.

Step 2: Restore the backed-up SUM directory.

Step 3: Start SUM using the restored backup.

Step 4: Execute the reset.

This ensures both the database and SUM state are returned to a consistent condition.

Manual Reset Procedure

In certain situations, you may need to perform a manual reset preparation.

Navigate to:

SUM/abap/bin

Execute:

SAPup reset prepare

Afterward:

  1. Stop all SAPup processes at OS level.
  2. Delete the SUM directory.
  3. Extract a fresh SUM package if needed.
  4. Start again from the beginning.

Common Mistake #1: Deleting the SUM Directory

Many administrators believe that deleting the SUM folder automatically resets the upgrade.

It does not.

SUM stores upgrade information inside the SAP system database.

When a new SUM package is extracted, it often reads the existing upgrade state and returns to the same phase.

Always perform a proper reset procedure rather than simply deleting SUM.

Common Mistake #2: Running Cleanup Too Early

After an update, SUM provides:

More → Cleanup

Be careful.

Once Cleanup has been executed:

  • Reset is no longer available
  • Rollback capability is removed

At that point, database restoration may be the only recovery option.

 

What If SUM Keeps Dropping Back into the Same Phase?

This happens when the Execution phase ran far enough to make changes to the system's upgrade versioning tables (UVERS), but not far enough to complete cleanly. Even after you clear the SUM directory and re-extract the SAR file, SUM reads those database tables and drops straight back into the last active phase.

This is not a bug. SUM is doing exactly what it's designed to do — resuming where it left off. The problem is that the state it's resuming from is inconsistent.

Ex: SAP Note 1790486 covers the scenario where SAP_ABA (or another software component) is in an undefined upgrade state that is not safe to proceed with.

In emergency situations, SAP may recommend running report RSUPGRES:

Important considerations:

  • Execute with DDIC
  • Use client 000
  • Run in background
  • Do not cancel the job manually

The report is intended to repair upgrade status inconsistencies and should be used carefully.

Note: RSUPGRES is a powerful and potentially destructive tool. Use it only as a last resort and follow every instruction. Do not improvise.

Afterward, many administrators also execute:

/$SYNC

and

/$TAB

to refresh buffers and synchronize system status.

 

A Word on Cleanup — Don't Confuse It with Reset

SUM has two end-of-process options that BASIS admins sometimes confuse:

Option

When It Appears

What It Does

Can You Reset After?

Reset

During the update process

Rolls back the upgrade, dissolves the shadow instance

Yes — but cleanup becomes mandatory afterward

Cleanup

After a successful update OR after a Reset

Removes SUM working files and state

No — reset is no longer possible after this

Note: Cleanup is a one-way door. Once you walk through it, you cannot come back without a full system restore. Always think twice before clicking it.

 

Key Takeaways

Most BASIS admins who've gone through a reset once develop a pre-Execution checklist they follow religiously. Here's what that looks like:

Before every upgrade:

✓ Take a full database backup

✓ Take a backup of the SUM directory

✓ Understand your rollback strategy

When an upgrade fails:

✓ Determine whether a SUM reset is still possible

✓ Do not immediately delete the SUM directory

✓ Consider the shadow instance impact

✓ Restore both the database and SUM state when necessary

Always remember that a successful rollback is not just about restoring the database. A complete recovery requires restoring upgrade consistency and ensuring the shadow instance is properly removed.

 

Have you run into a reset scenario that isn't covered here?

Drop it in the comments.

entry.title: Top 10 Best Practices for SAP Sustainability Control Tower
entry.content:
Top 10 Best Practices for SAP Sustainability Control Tower

  1. Establish Cross-Functional Governance: Form a steering committee including Sustainability, Finance, and IT. Define clear data ownership across business units before configuring the software.

  2. Adopt the "Finance-First" Mindset: Treat your sustainability data exactly like financial data. Implement strict internal controls and validation checks to ensure audit-ready ESG reporting.

  3. Automate ERP Integration: Eliminate manual data uploads via files. Use standard APIs to connect SCT directly with SAP S/4HANA and SAP SuccessFactors for real-time visibility.

  4. Leverage Supplier Data Exchange: For Scope 3 emissions, avoid generic industry averages. Use SAP SDX to receive actual product footprint data from supply chain partners.

  5. Master Compliance Frameworks Early: Pre-align your existing corporate data models with built-in SCT content for major frameworks like CSRD and EU Taxonomy.

  6. Stick to Clean Core Strategy: Keep your integration architecture clean. Avoid custom middleware inside SCT and utilize SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) if extensions are needed.

  7. Start with a Focused Scope: Adopt a phased approach. Begin with high-priority, readily available metrics (like Scope 1 and 2) before scaling to complex Scope 3 KPIs.

  8. Define Validation Protocols: Establish clear rules for handling messy or anomalous data. Know exactly who is responsible for fixing source data errors before year-end audits.

  9. Utilize Performance Forecasting: Don't just look back. Use built-in target setting and "What-if" simulations to model sustainability scenarios and drive strategic decisions.

  10. Invest in Continuous Training: Educate operational teams on how accurate data entry at the source (like logging energy bills in S/4HANA) impacts global compliance.

Why S/4HANA API Automation Matters

The Architecture Reality: Transitioning to automated S/4HANA APIs is non-negotiable for establishing a finance-grade "Green Ledger." It eliminates human errors inherent in manual spreadsheet updates, secures automated data lineage for third-party auditors, and enables continuous, real-time performance steering rather than lagging retrospective reporting.

entry.title: Re: Custom “Final Salary” field – how to derive cumulativ...
entry.content:
This is expected behavior.  For example, if you have 5 employees and each receive a 3% increase, the calculated total function would in fact produce 15%, not the 3% you would expect.  the total is a summation of the column not a calculation within the total row.

My thought for getting a percentage would be to use a budget.  You could either have the proposed salary compared to the current salary or use the % of a custom field.   That way you could see the percentage increase in total.   I think you may need to configure and then tweak to get exactly what you want.
